subject: ap-world-history
title: Short Answer Question — AP World History

parts.prompt:
Identify ONE historical development that contributed to the growth of a global economy during the
period circa 1800–1914.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying ONE historical development that contributed to the growth of a global economy circa 1800–1914. AP-acceptable responses: the Industrial Revolution (steam-powered production and transport multiplying output and reducing transport costs); construction of canals (Suez 1869, Panama 1914) shortening oceanic trade routes; railroad networks integrating continental interiors with ports; the telegraph providing near-instant long-distance price information; European imperial expansion creating coerced integration of Asian and African economies into global markets; adoption of the gold standard (British lead) providing a stable international monetary framework.

sample_responses.response: The opening of the Suez Canal in 1869 dramatically shortened the sea route between Europe and South/East Asia — eliminating the long voyage around the Cape of Good Hope and cutting weeks from shipping times — which greatly increased the volume and profitability of European–Asian trade and accelerated the growth of a truly global economy in the late 19th century.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific infrastructure development with a date and explains its mechanism for expanding the global economy. Historically accurate.

sample_responses.response: The rapid construction of railroad networks across North America, Europe, Russia, India, and parts of South America between the 1830s and 1914 integrated agricultural and mining hinterlands with ports — moving grain from the US Great Plains to Atlantic ports and wheat from Russian Ukraine to Odessa at costs that transformed global agricultural markets.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific development (railroad networks) with explicit global-economy mechanism (integrating hinterlands with export ports). Historically accurate.
Describes the outcome without identifying a specific development that drove it.

required_elements: Historically defensible response addressing the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: Historically defensible response addressing the prompt.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain ONE way governments responded to economic crises or depressions during the period circa
1900–1945.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining ONE way governments responded to economic crises or depressions circa 1900–1945. AP-acceptable responses: US Franklin Roosevelt's New Deal (1933+) — Social Security, Works Progress Administration, banking reforms — in response to the Great Depression; Nazi Germany's autarky and public-works spending on Autobahns and rearmament; British abandonment of the gold standard (1931); Soviet Union's Five-Year Plans and collectivization (1928+) as a centrally planned alternative; Japan's move toward state-directed heavy industry and military-economic planning; protectionist tariff responses such as the US Smoot–Hawley Tariff (1930).

sample_responses.response: In response to the Great Depression, the US government under Franklin Roosevelt launched the New Deal (1933+) — a package of banking reforms (Glass–Steagall), public-works employment (the Works Progress Administration, Civilian Conservation Corps), and social insurance (Social Security Act, 1935) — shifting the US government toward a much larger role in managing economic stability.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a CB-standard response (New Deal) with specific program examples and a clear causal link to the Depression. Historically accurate.

sample_responses.response: Nazi Germany responded to the Depression with public-works projects (the Autobahn network) and massive rearmament spending — directed through state-controlled industrial policy — which reduced unemployment dramatically while simultaneously preparing the German economy for war.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a CB-standard response (Nazi state economic intervention + rearmament) with specific mechanisms. Historically accurate.
Does not explain any specific policy response.

required_elements: Historically defensible response addressing the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: Historically defensible response addressing the prompt.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.

Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain ONE way states changed their economies in response to the shifting global balance of power
during the period circa 1945–1990.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining ONE way states changed their economies in response to the shifting global balance of power circa 1945–1990. AP-acceptable responses: Soviet Union and Eastern Bloc's command economies and COMECON integration; Western European reconstruction through the Marshall Plan and formation of the European Economic Community; Japanese MITI-led export-oriented industrialization (the 'Japanese miracle'); Asian Tigers (South Korea, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Singapore) pursuing state-led export growth; China's shift from Maoist autarky to Deng Xiaoping's reform-and-opening after 1978; newly independent states pursuing import-substitution industrialization (Nehruvian India); OPEC's coordinated oil pricing after 1973.

sample_responses.response: China, after Mao's death, pivoted sharply under Deng Xiaoping beginning in 1978 — establishing Special Economic Zones in Shenzhen, Zhuhai, and elsewhere that permitted foreign direct investment and market-based production — a decisive response to the post-Cultural-Revolution weakness of the Chinese economy and to the example of neighboring export-oriented Asian economies.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a specific economic change (China's Reform and Opening) with concrete mechanisms (SEZs, FDI) and ties it to the shifting power balance. Historically accurate.

sample_responses.response: South Korea and Taiwan, facing Cold War pressure and aligned with the US-led bloc, pursued export-oriented industrialization through state-directed development banking and planning — channeling capital into chaebol conglomerates (Korea) and state-owned heavy industry (Taiwan) to transform themselves from low-income economies in the 1950s to major industrial exporters by the 1980s.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a specific economic strategy (state-led export-oriented industrialization) with country-specific examples tied to the Cold War power balance.
Too vague; does not identify a specific change or state.
